enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source XMPP/Jabber chat client written in JavaScript that runs entirely in the browser, embeddable as a widget or deployed as a full-page messaging app.
enrichment.seo_title: Converse.js: Open-Source XMPP Web Chat Client
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Converse.js is a free, open-source XMPP/Jabber chat client for the browser. Embed real-time messaging with OMEMO encryption and group chats on any site.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Converse.js is a free and open-source, browser-based chat client for the XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol, also known as Jabber) instant-messaging protocol. Written entirely in JavaScript, it runs client-side and connects to any standards-compliant XMPP server, letting developers add real-time one-on-one chat, multi-user group chats (MUC), and presence features to a website without building a messaging backend from scratch. The project was created and has been primarily maintained by South African developer JC Brand, with development beginning around 2012-2013. It is released under the Mozilla Public License version 2 (MPLv2), a permissive-but-copyleft license that allows commercial and non-commercial use, modification, and embedding while requiring that modifications to Converse's own source files remain open. The project is community-driven and sponsored through Open Collective and corporate backers rather than being a venture-funded startup; there is no company behind it in the traditional sense, and it has no per-seat or subscription pricing because the software itself is entirely free to self-host and use. Architecturally, Converse.js is a single-page application built with a plugin architecture (based on the pluggable.js library) that lets developers enable or disable features and write custom extensions. It ships with a full-page chat application out of the box but is most commonly deployed in one of three modes: an overlaid chat bubble similar to a customer-support widget, an embedded widget placed inside an existing page layout, or a standalone full-page messaging client. Because XMPP is a federated, open protocol (the same technology underlying WhatsApp's original architecture and Jitsi), Converse.js works with any compliant server implementation, including popular self-hosted options such as ejabberd, Prosody, and Openfire, as well as hosted XMPP providers. Key differentiators include first-class support for modern XMPP Extension Protocols (XEPs): OMEMO end-to-end encryption (XEP-0384) for private conversations and encrypted file/media sharing (XEP-0454), message styling (XEP-0393, Markdown-like formatting), message correction and retraction, moderator tools for group chats, message carbons and archive management (MAM) for multi-device history sync, and push notification support. The interface has been translated into more than 40 languages by community contributors, making it suitable for international deployments. Converse.js targets a technical audience: developers and organizations who want to self-host a chat feature rather than depend on a proprietary SaaS messaging vendor, particularly those already running or willing to run XMPP infrastructure. Typical use cases include embedding live chat/support widgets on websites, building internal team communication tools on top of self-hosted infrastructure, powering community or forum chat rooms, and providing a privacy-respecting alternative to closed messaging platforms for NGOs, cooperatives, and privacy-focused organizations. Because it is open protocol and open source end-to-end (client and, optionally, server), it appeals strongly to users who prioritize data sovereignty, self-hosting, and avoiding vendor lock-in — a very different value proposition from closed, hosted chat widgets like Intercom or Crisp, or from centralized messaging platforms like Slack or Discord. Within the XMPP ecosystem specifically, Converse.js competes with other web-based XMPP clients such as Movim, JSXC, and Candy Chat, though Converse is generally regarded as the most actively maintained and feature-complete browser-based option. More broadly, it is sometimes evaluated alongside other self-hostable, protocol-based chat systems such as Matrix/Element (which uses the Matrix protocol rather than XMPP) and Rocket.Chat. Deployment is lightweight: Converse.js can be dropped into a page via a script tag and a small configuration object specifying the XMPP connection endpoint (BOSH or WebSocket), authentication method (including anonymous login support for public chat rooms), and which plugins to enable — there is no build step or backend server required for the client itself, only an XMPP server to connect to. A companion project, converse-desktop, wraps the web client in Electron to provide native-feeling desktop apps for macOS, Windows, and Linux. Because Converse.js is not a company, it has no funding history, revenue model, headquarters, or enterprise sales process; its sustainability comes from GitHub sponsorships, Open Collective donations, and paid support/customization arrangements JC Brand and contributors offer on a freelance basis. For a directory or comparison context, Converse.js should be positioned as the leading open-source, self-hosted browser chat client for the XMPP protocol — a developer tool rather than a turnkey SaaS product — appealing to teams that want full control over their messaging data and infrastructure and are comfortable running or provisioning an XMPP server alongside it. In practice, Converse.js is frequently referenced in self-hosting and homelab communities (such as discussions on the Cloudron app platform forums) as one of the easiest ways to add a chat feature to a self-managed application stack, and step-by-step deployment guides exist for pairing it with popular hosting platforms. It supports anonymous, credential-free logins for public multi-user chat rooms, which makes it viable as a lightweight, ephemeral support-chat widget in addition to persistent, authenticated one-on-one messaging. Because the client speaks the open XMPP protocol over either BOSH (long-polling HTTP) or native WebSocket connections, it can be pointed at virtually any XMPP deployment without vendor-specific client code, which is a structural advantage over closed, single-vendor chat widget SDKs whose client libraries only work with that vendor's backend.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Converse.js is a free, open-source chat client for the XMPP (Jabber) protocol that runs entirely in the browser. Rather than shipping a hosted messaging service, it gives developers a JavaScript library they can embed on any website and connect to a standards-compliant XMPP server they control.

First developed around 2012-2013 by JC Brand and released under the Mozilla Public License v2, Converse.js has grown into the most actively maintained web-based XMPP client available, with a plugin architecture, translations into 40+ languages, and support for modern encryption and messaging extensions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Converse.js supports one-on-one chat, multi-user chat rooms (MUC), OMEMO end-to-end encryption for both text and shared files, message correction and retraction, moderator and admin tooling for group chats, and multi-device history sync through Message Archive Management (MAM).

It can be deployed as an overlaid chat bubble, an embedded widget inside a page, or a full-page standalone messaging app, and it works with any XMPP server, including self-hosted options like ejabberd, Prosody, and Openfire.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Converse.js is completely free to use, modify, and self-host under the MPLv2 license. There are no subscription tiers, seat limits, or paid upgrades for the client itself; costs are limited to whatever infrastructure is used to run the XMPP server it connects to.

Because the project is community-maintained rather than a commercial company, support is available through community channels, GitHub issues, and paid freelance arrangements with contributors rather than a formal enterprise sales or support tier.

faqs.question: Is Converse.js free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Converse.js is fully open source under the Mozilla Public License v2 and free to use, self-host, and modify with no subscription cost.

faqs.question: Do I need my own XMPP server to use Converse.js?
faqs.answer: Yes. Converse.js is only the browser-based client; it must connect to a standards-compliant XMPP server such as ejabberd, Prosody, Openfire, or a hosted XMPP provider.

faqs.question: Does Converse.js support encrypted messaging?
faqs.answer: Yes. It supports OMEMO end-to-end encryption for one-on-one chats and encrypted file sharing via XEP-0454.

faqs.question: Can Converse.js be embedded as a support widget?
faqs.answer: Yes. It can run as an overlaid chat bubble or embedded widget in addition to a full-page chat application.

faqs.question: Is there a desktop app version?
faqs.answer: Yes, via the companion converse-desktop project, which wraps the web client in Electron for macOS, Windows, and Linux.

faqs.question: Who maintains Converse.js?
faqs.answer: It is maintained primarily by creator JC Brand along with community contributors, funded through Open Collective and sponsorships rather than a commercial company.

enrichment.short_description: Convex is a reactive backend-as-a-service platform combining a real-time document database, serverless TypeScript functions, auth, and file storage for full-stack app developers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Convex is a reactive backend platform with a real-time database, serverless TypeScript functions, auth, and file storage built for modern full-stack apps.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Convex is a backend-as-a-service (BaaS) platform built around a reactive document database and serverless TypeScript functions, designed to let full-stack developers build real-time, data-driven applications without assembling separate database, API, auth, and caching layers themselves. The company was founded in 2021 by Jamie Turner (CEO), James Cowling (CTO), and Sujay Jayakar, three engineers who previously worked together at Dropbox on large-scale distributed storage and database systems; James Cowling holds a PhD from MIT focused on distributed transaction processing and consensus protocols. Convex is headquartered in San Francisco and has raised venture funding across multiple rounds, including a Series A of roughly $25.7 million led by Andreessen Horowitz with participation from Netlify and Elad Gil, a Series B led by Fifth Wall, Emergence Capital, and GGV Capital, and an additional $24 million round led by a16z and co-led by Spark Capital in late 2025 with participation from Neo, Alumni Ventures, and angel investors including Adam D'Angelo and Drew Houston. Architecturally, Convex centers on a reactive database where queries are TypeScript (or JavaScript) functions that automatically re-run and push updated results to connected clients whenever underlying data changes, eliminating the need for developers to hand-write WebSocket subscriptions, cache invalidation logic, or polling. Application logic is written as three function types deployed directly on Convex's serverless infrastructure: queries (read-only, cached, and reactive), mutations (transactional writes with ACID guarantees), and actions (for calling external APIs or running non-deterministic code). The platform bundles complementary building blocks that would otherwise require separate vendors: built-in authentication integrations (commonly paired with Clerk or Auth0), file storage, scheduled and cron functions, full-text and vector search for AI and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) workloads, and end-to-end TypeScript type safety from the database schema through to the client. A significant differentiator announced in 2024 is that Convex open-sourced its core backend (convex-backend) under the Apache 2.0 license on GitHub, allowing developers to self-host the engine, while the primary commercial product remains the managed cloud offering. Convex's pricing follows a freemium, usage-based structure. The Free (Starter) plan is scale-to-zero and pay-as-you-go, offering generous built-in limits suitable for prototypes, side projects, and early-stage apps, including a fixed monthly allotment of function calls and database storage before usage-based billing kicks in; teams can have multiple free collaborators. The Professional plan uses per-developer seat pricing (around $25 per developer per month) combined with higher built-in resource allowances — larger database storage, I/O, and bandwidth quotas — and lower per-unit overage rates once those built-in limits are exceeded. An Enterprise tier, in active development at the time of research, is aimed at larger organizations needing advanced telemetry, compliance certifications, single sign-on, audit logging, and formal SLAs. Convex also runs a startup program offering up to a year of free Professional-tier access (with no seat fees and discounted usage fees up to a capped amount) to qualifying early-stage companies, a common go-to-market tactic among developer-infrastructure vendors competing for the indie-hacker and YC-adjacent developer audience. Convex's target users are primarily full-stack web and mobile developers — particularly those building with React, Next.js, React Native, and other TypeScript-first frameworks — who want a single reactive backend rather than manually wiring together Postgres, Redis, a pub/sub layer, and a serverless function host. It has also positioned itself for AI-native application development, given its native vector search and support for building agentic workflows with persistent, reactive state. In the competitive landscape, Convex sits alongside other backend-as-a-service and reactive-data platforms such as Firebase (Google), Supabase, PocketBase, and Appwrite, as well as more narrowly-scoped real-time sync engines like Firebase Realtime Database or Ably; it differentiates itself from Supabase and Firebase specifically through its TypeScript-native function model, automatic reactivity without manual subscription wiring, and transactional consistency guarantees on writes. Because the core database engine is open source, Convex also invites comparison to self-hostable backend stacks, even though most users adopt the managed cloud service for its zero-ops convenience. Notable facts include Convex's origin story rooted in the founders' shared experience building and operating Dropbox's storage infrastructure at scale, its early positioning under the tagline of getting developers to 'dump databases' in favor of a higher-level reactive data layer, and its growing emphasis on serving AI agent and RAG-style workloads as a differentiator versus older BaaS incumbents. For a SaaS directory audience, Convex should be understood as a modern, TypeScript-centric alternative to traditional database-plus-API-plus-auth stacks, most relevant to developers building real-time, collaborative, or AI-powered applications who want to minimize backend infrastructure decisions. Note that the name 'Convex' is shared by an unrelated commercial-insurance technology company (Convex Insurance), so buyers researching this backend-as-a-service platform should confirm they are looking at convex.dev and the Jamie Turner/James Cowling/Sujay Jayakar team rather than that separate, unrelated business. Convex's documentation and marketing emphasize a 'sync engine' mental model: rather than developers thinking in terms of REST endpoints and manual cache invalidation, they think in terms of declarative queries that Convex keeps continuously fresh, which is the platform's central pitch to teams evaluating it against hand-rolled Postgres-plus-tRPC-plus-WebSocket stacks. The company continues to invest heavily in its startup and founder-focused go-to-market motion, offering free Professional-tier credits specifically to reduce the up-front cost of adoption for early-stage teams building on React, Next.js, and React Native.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Convex is a reactive backend-as-a-service platform that bundles a real-time document database, serverless TypeScript functions, authentication, file storage, and search into a single managed system. It was founded in 2021 by ex-Dropbox engineers Jamie Turner, James Cowling, and Sujay Jayakar and is headquartered in San Francisco.

Instead of manually wiring together a database, an API layer, and real-time subscriptions, developers write queries and mutations as TypeScript functions; Convex automatically keeps connected clients in sync whenever the underlying data changes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include reactive queries that auto-update connected UIs, ACID-transactional mutations, scheduled functions and cron jobs, built-in file storage, vector and full-text search for AI/RAG use cases, and end-to-end TypeScript type safety from schema to client.

Convex integrates with popular auth providers like Clerk and Auth0, and its core backend engine is open source under Apache 2.0, giving teams the option to self-host while most use the managed cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Convex offers a free, pay-as-you-go Starter plan with generous built-in resource limits ideal for prototypes and small projects, and a Professional plan priced around $25 per developer per month that unlocks larger resource allowances and lower usage overage rates.

An Enterprise plan aimed at larger organizations, with SSO, compliance features, and SLAs, is available for teams with advanced requirements, and qualifying startups can apply for a year of free Professional access.

faqs.question: What is Convex used for?
faqs.answer: Convex is a backend-as-a-service platform used to build real-time, data-driven web and mobile applications with a reactive database, serverless functions, auth, and storage in one system.

faqs.question: Is Convex open source?
faqs.answer: The core Convex backend engine (convex-backend) is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted, though most users run the managed cloud service.

faqs.question: How much does Convex cost?
faqs.answer: Convex has a free, pay-as-you-go Starter plan and a Professional plan priced around $25 per developer per month with higher resource allowances; an Enterprise tier with custom pricing is also available.

faqs.question: What languages does Convex support?
faqs.answer: Convex functions are written in TypeScript or JavaScript, with official client libraries for React, Next.js, React Native, and other JavaScript frameworks.

faqs.question: How is Convex different from Firebase or Supabase?
faqs.answer: Convex emphasizes automatic reactivity without manual subscription code, ACID-transactional writes, and a TypeScript-native function model, whereas Firebase and Supabase use different data and consistency models.

faqs.question: Who founded Convex?
faqs.answer: Convex was founded in 2021 by Jamie Turner, James Cowling, and Sujay Jayakar, former Dropbox engineers who worked on large-scale storage and database systems.

enrichment.short_description: Convoy is an open-source, cloud-native webhooks gateway that securely ingests, retries, and delivers webhook events at scale for engineering teams.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Convoy is an open-source, cloud-native webhooks gateway for reliable event delivery, with retries, rate limiting, and self-hosted or cloud deployment.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Convoy is an open-source, cloud-native webhooks gateway built to solve the infrastructure problem of reliably sending and receiving webhook events at scale. It is developed by Frain Technologies (also referred to by its GitHub organization name, frain-dev) and was founded in 2021 by co-founders Subomi Oluwalana (CEO) and Emmanuel Aina, who previously worked together as engineers at Tangerine Africa and other local and international startups before building an API product for fintech companies; repeated customer requests for reliable webhook infrastructure while selling that earlier product directly inspired them to build Convoy. The company went through Y Combinator's Winter 2022 (W22) batch and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with part of its engineering team based in Lagos, Nigeria. Convoy is written in Go, the same language used to build infrastructure staples like Kubernetes and Prometheus, and is designed to sit at the edge of a company's network as a dedicated webhooks layer: on the outbound side, it ingests events from internal microservices and reliably fans them out to customer-configured endpoints; on the inbound side, it can receive webhooks from third-party providers (such as Stripe or Twilio) and route them to internal services, shielding those internal systems from direct public internet exposure. Core reliability features include automatic retries with configurable backoff, rate limiting per endpoint, circuit breaking to avoid hammering failing endpoints, static outbound IP addresses for allow-listing, rolling/rotating signing secrets for webhook signature verification, event replay and debugging tools, and dead-letter-style handling for events that ultimately cannot be delivered. Convoy has always shipped its core engine as open source with a public roadmap, though its licensing has evolved over time: it originally used the Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0) and later moved toward the Elastic License 2.0 (ELv2) for parts of its codebase, an open-core licensing pattern common among infrastructure startups that need to prevent large cloud providers from directly re-hosting their software as a competing managed service while still keeping the core project source-available and free for self-hosting. Convoy is distributed in three primary ways: a free, self-hosted Community edition that developers can run on their own infrastructure at no license cost; a fully managed Convoy Cloud offering with usage-based, transparent pricing aimed at small teams and startups, with published Cloud pricing starting around $99 per month for a Pro-tier plan; and an Enterprise Partnership Program for large-scale organizations processing more than roughly 20 million webhook events per month, which includes a dedicated customer success team, custom pricing, and a licensed Premium/self-hosted tier obtained through a sales conversation. Convoy's target users are backend and platform engineering teams at companies that either send large volumes of outbound webhooks to their own customers (SaaS platforms, fintechs, e-commerce platforms) or need to reliably ingest and route inbound webhooks from third-party vendors. Because reliable webhook delivery is a deceptively hard distributed-systems problem — requiring retry logic, ordering guarantees, security, observability, and scaling — many engineering teams historically built ad hoc, fragile in-house solutions; Convoy packages this as dedicated, battle-tested infrastructure instead. In the competitive landscape, Convoy competes with other dedicated webhooks-infrastructure vendors such as Svix, Hookdeck, and Novu (for outbound event delivery specifically), as well as broader event-streaming and message-queue platforms like AWS EventBridge, Kafka-based solutions, and various API gateway products that offer webhook-adjacent functionality as a secondary feature. Its open-source, self-hostable nature differentiates it from fully closed SaaS competitors like Svix, appealing particularly to teams with data residency, compliance, or cost-control requirements that make self-hosting attractive, while its managed Cloud option and enterprise support tier let it also compete for customers who prefer a fully outsourced solution. Notable facts include Convoy's origin as a spin-off need discovered while building fintech API infrastructure in the Nigerian and broader African tech ecosystem, its Y Combinator W22 batch membership alongside a large cohort of African startups, and its commitment to keeping a public roadmap and open-source core even as it introduced commercial licensing terms for certain advanced or enterprise features. For a SaaS directory audience, Convoy should be positioned as a specialized, open-source-first webhooks gateway for engineering teams that need production-grade reliability for outbound and inbound event delivery, offering a self-hosted free tier, an accessible paid Cloud plan for smaller teams, and custom enterprise pricing for high-volume organizations. The company has publicly emphasized keeping a generous free tier for individual developers even as it introduced paid Cloud and Enterprise tiers, framing this as a deliberate choice to keep Convoy accessible to the same kind of early-stage, resource-constrained engineering teams its founders once were. Convoy also publishes technical content through its 'Webhooks Blog,' covering both product updates and general webhooks engineering best practices, which it uses to build credibility and inbound interest with the backend engineering audience it targets. Its GitHub repository (frain-dev/convoy) remains the canonical distribution channel for the self-hosted Community edition, and the project maintains a public roadmap so prospective self-hosters and Cloud customers alike can track upcoming feature work before committing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Convoy is an open-source, cloud-native webhooks gateway that helps engineering teams reliably send and receive webhook events at scale. Built in Go and founded in 2021 by Subomi Oluwalana and Emmanuel Aina, Convoy went through Y Combinator's W22 batch and is headquartered in San Francisco with engineering based partly in Lagos, Nigeria.

The platform sits at the edge of a company's infrastructure, ingesting outbound events from internal services and delivering them to external endpoints, while also routing inbound webhooks from third-party providers to internal systems without exposing them directly to the public internet.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Convoy provides automatic retries with configurable backoff, per-endpoint rate limiting, circuit breaking, static outbound IPs, rolling signing secrets, and event replay and debugging tools to make webhook delivery reliable and observable.
It is available as a free, self-hosted Community edition, a managed Convoy Cloud offering, and an Enterprise Partnership Program for organizations processing very high volumes of events.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing

Organizations sending more than roughly 20 million webhook events per month qualify for Convoy's Enterprise Partnership Program, which includes custom pricing and dedicated customer success support.

faqs.question: Is Convoy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Convoy's core engine is open source and free to self-host. A managed Convoy Cloud plan starts at roughly $99/month, and an Enterprise tier is available for high-volume customers.

faqs.question: What is Convoy used for?
faqs.answer: Convoy is a webhooks gateway used to reliably send outbound webhook events to customers and to securely receive and route inbound webhooks from third-party providers.

faqs.question: Is Convoy open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Convoy has always shipped its core as open source with a public roadmap, though some newer components use the Elastic License 2.0 rather than a fully permissive license.

faqs.question: What programming language is Convoy built in?
faqs.answer: Convoy is written in Go, chosen for performance and reliability at the scale required for infrastructure software.

faqs.question: Who founded Convoy?
faqs.answer: Convoy was founded in 2021 by Subomi Oluwalana and Emmanuel Aina, and it went through Y Combinator's Winter 2022 (W22) batch.

faqs.question: How is Convoy different from the trucking company Convoy?
faqs.answer: This Convoy (getconvoy.io) is an unrelated, separate open-source webhooks infrastructure company; it has no connection to the digital freight/trucking network of the same name.

enrichment.short_description: Coolify is an open-source, self-hostable PaaS alternative to Heroku, Vercel, and Netlify for deploying apps, databases, and services on your own servers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coolify is a free, open-source, self-hostable alternative to Heroku, Vercel, and Netlify for deploying apps, databases, and 280+ services on your servers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Coolify is an open-source, self-hostable platform-as-a-service (PaaS) that lets developers deploy static sites, full-stack applications, databases, and pre-packaged one-click services onto their own servers, positioning itself as a self-hosted alternative to Heroku, Vercel, and Netlify. It was created by Hungarian developer Andras Bacsai, who started Coolify as a side project in 2021 during the early pandemic period and eventually left full-time employment to work on it, growing it into what is widely regarded as the most popular self-hosted PaaS tool in the open-source space. The project is developed by coolLabs Solutions Kft., a bootstrapped, independent company based in Székesfehérvár, Hungary, that has taken no venture capital funding and has no American parent company, giving it full control over its roadmap and business model. Coolify's core value proposition is operational simplicity combined with infrastructure ownership: a developer connects a Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, or self-hosted Git), clicks deploy, and Coolify handles the rest of the deployment pipeline automatically. Under the hood, it builds applications using Nixpacks (an auto-detecting buildpack system) or a custom Dockerfile, configures a Traefik reverse proxy for routing, provisions automatic SSL/TLS certificates via Let's Encrypt, and can provision managed databases including PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, Redis, and MongoDB directly within the platform. It supports preview deployments tied to Git branches or pull requests, scheduled backups to any S3-compatible object storage, environment variable and secrets management, and a marketplace of more than 280 one-click service templates covering popular open-source tools (analytics platforms, CMSs, databases, monitoring stacks, and more) that can be deployed with a single click rather than manual Docker Compose configuration. The entire platform is released under the Apache 2.0 license and is completely free to self-host, with no artificial feature gating, no user or team caps, and no trial period restrictions — the full product, including all 280+ templates, unlimited servers, and unlimited team members, is available at zero software cost. The only expense in self-hosting is the underlying infrastructure itself: Coolify runs on Docker and can manage any number of connected VPS or bare-metal servers from providers such as Hetzner, DigitalOcean, AWS, or Linode, typically costing between roughly $5 and $25 per month depending on server size and provider. For teams that don't want to self-host the Coolify control plane itself, the company also offers Coolify Cloud, a paid managed version of the orchestration layer (not the application hosting itself) starting at $5 per month for two connected servers, with additional servers priced around $3 per month each and roughly a 20% discount for annual billing; customers using Cloud still bring and pay for their own underlying servers from any provider, with Coolify Cloud managing the coordination, dashboard, and deployment automation across them. On the self-hosted edition, teams and team members are unlimited at no additional cost, letting organizations create separate teams per client or project with distinct server access and permissions within a single Coolify instance; on Coolify Cloud, each additional team generally requires its own separate Cloud subscription. Coolify's target users are developers, small teams, agencies, and self-hosting enthusiasts who want the developer experience of a modern PaaS (git-push deploys, automatic HTTPS, managed databases, preview environments) without paying per-app or per-usage fees to a commercial cloud platform, and without giving up control of where their application data and infrastructure physically reside. This makes it especially popular with indie hackers, bootstrapped startups, digital agencies managing many client projects, and organizations with data-residency or cost-control concerns about hyperscale cloud PaaS pricing. In the competitive landscape, Coolify's closest peers are other self-hosted PaaS and deployment orchestration tools such as CapRover, Dokku, and Sealos, as well as commercial managed platforms it explicitly positions against — Heroku, Vercel, Netlify, and Railway — the key distinction being that Coolify's core software is free and runs on infrastructure the user owns, rather than usage-metered infrastructure owned by the vendor. Notable facts include Coolify's rapid organic growth almost entirely through open-source community adoption and word of mouth rather than paid marketing, its status as a bootstrapped, VC-free company at a time when many competing infrastructure startups are venture-funded, and its reputation within the self-hosting and homelab community as one of the most polished and actively developed projects in that space, with frequent releases (Coolify v4 introduced substantial architecture and UI improvements over earlier major versions). For a SaaS directory audience, Coolify should be framed as a free, open-source, developer-first deployment platform aimed at anyone who wants Heroku-like or Vercel-like ease of deployment while retaining full ownership of their servers and data, with an optional low-cost Cloud tier for teams that want a managed control plane without giving up server ownership. Coolify's project sustainability comes from a mix of Coolify Cloud subscription revenue, Open Collective donations, and GitHub sponsorships, a funding model that mirrors other successful bootstrapped open-source infrastructure projects rather than the venture-backed 'open core' model used by some competitors. The coolLabs team publishes its roadmap and contributor list openly, and the project has built a reputation for shipping frequent releases and responding quickly to community-reported issues, which is frequently cited as a differentiator versus other self-hosted PaaS tools that see slower release cadences. Coolify's growing marketplace of one-click templates is community-contributed as well as maintained by the core team, meaning the catalog of deployable services continues to expand as the self-hosting community adds new templates.

enrichment.short_description: Copper is a CRM built natively for Google Workspace, automatically capturing Gmail, Calendar, and Drive activity to manage sales pipelines with minimal manual data entry.
enrichment.seo_title: Copper CRM: The Native Google Workspace CRM
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Copper is a CRM built for Google Workspace, auto-capturing Gmail and Calendar activity to run sales pipelines with less manual data entry. Plans from $23/user.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Copper is a customer relationship management (CRM) platform purpose-built to integrate natively with Google Workspace (Gmail, Calendar, Drive, and Sheets), designed to minimize the manual data entry that plagues typical CRM adoption by automatically capturing contacts, email threads, meetings, and related activity directly from a user's existing Google account. The company was originally founded in 2013 in San Francisco under the name ProsperWorks by Jon Lee, and rebranded to Copper in 2018 as it broadened its focus beyond a narrow Google-Chrome-extension product into a full CRM platform while retaining deep, native Google Workspace integration as its core differentiator. Copper is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and has raised a total of approximately $99.5 million in venture funding across six rounds from investors including Norwest Venture Partners and Industry Ventures, reaching Series C funding status. The company reports serving more than 30,000 businesses and is recognized as a Google-recommended CRM listed prominently in the Google Workspace Marketplace, reflecting a close product and go-to-market partnership with Google. Architecturally, Copper positions itself as a CRM that lives inside Gmail and the broader Google Workspace interface rather than requiring reps to switch context into a separate application: it automatically logs emails, calls, and calendar events against the correct contact or company record, suggests new contacts and leads based on email activity, and syncs bidirectionally with Google Contacts and Calendar. Beyond the Google integration, Copper provides standard CRM capabilities including customizable sales pipelines and deal stages, task and workflow automation to reduce manual follow-up work, lead and opportunity tracking, reporting and forecasting dashboards, email templates and mail merge, and a Chrome extension that surfaces CRM context directly inside the Gmail inbox. It also offers a Copper CRM for Google Sheets integration, letting teams view and edit CRM data from within a spreadsheet interface, and supports integrations with tools like Slack, Zoom, QuickBooks, and various marketing automation platforms via its API and app marketplace. Copper's pricing is structured around three core paid tiers with no permanent free plan, though a 14-day free trial requiring no credit card is available. The Basic plan is priced at $23 per user per month when billed annually (or $29 per user per month billed monthly) and supports up to 2,500 contacts with a limit of 25 custom fields, aimed at small teams that need core pipeline management and task automation. The Professional plan, generally described as Copper's most popular tier, costs $59 per user per month billed annually (or $69 monthly) and raises the contact limit to 15,000 with 50 custom fields, adding more advanced automation and reporting capabilities. The Business plan starts at $99 per user per month billed annually (or $134 monthly), removes the contact cap entirely, and is designed for larger, more established teams that need full customization, advanced reporting, and broader integration options. Annual billing offers meaningful savings — up to roughly 26% — compared to month-to-month pricing across all tiers. Copper's target users are small and mid-size businesses (SMBs) that already run their business operations on Google Workspace and want a CRM that fits into that existing workflow rather than forcing a switch to a Microsoft- or Salesforce-centric ecosystem; it's particularly popular among agencies, consultancies, and relationship-driven B2B sales teams that value low-friction adoption and minimal manual data entry over the deep customizability of enterprise CRM suites. In the competitive CRM landscape, Copper is most frequently compared to HubSpot CRM, Pipedrive, Streak (another Gmail-native CRM), Salesforce Essentials/Starter, and monday.com's sales CRM; its distinguishing position relative to these competitors is its unusually deep, native Google Workspace integration and its lower learning curve for teams already living inside Gmail day-to-day, as opposed to Salesforce's greater configurability and enterprise feature depth or HubSpot's broader all-in-one marketing/sales/service suite. Notable facts include Copper's rebrand from ProsperWorks in 2018 signaling a strategic shift from a Chrome-extension-first product to a standalone SaaS platform, its official Google-recommended CRM status within the Workspace Marketplace, and its reported base of more than 30,000 customer businesses. For a SaaS directory audience, Copper should be framed as the leading CRM choice specifically for Google Workspace-native teams — organizations for whom 'does it work seamlessly with Gmail and Calendar' is a primary CRM selection criterion — rather than as a general-purpose CRM competing on raw customization depth with Salesforce or as an all-in-one marketing suite competing with HubSpot. Copper is particularly popular in relationship-driven verticals such as real estate, consulting, marketing and creative agencies, and professional services firms, where deal cycles are built around ongoing email correspondence and meetings rather than high-volume, transactional outbound selling. Beyond its native Google integrations, Copper connects to broader business tools such as Slack, Zoom, QuickBooks Online, DocuSign, and various marketing automation and e-signature platforms through its API and app marketplace, letting growing teams extend the CRM without abandoning their Google Workspace-centric workflow. Independent review platforms such as G2 and Capterra generally rate Copper highly on ease of use and Google integration specifically, while noting that its customization depth and advanced sales-forecasting capabilities trail more complex, enterprise-oriented CRMs.

enrichment.short_description: Copy.ai is an AI-powered content and workflow automation platform for sales and marketing teams, generating copy, chat responses, and automated GTM workflows.
enrichment.seo_title: Copy.ai: AI Content and Sales Workflow Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Copy.ai is an AI writing and workflow automation platform for sales and marketing, offering chat, brand voice training, and automated GTM workflows.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Copy.ai is an AI-powered content generation and go-to-market (GTM) workflow automation platform originally built around AI copywriting and later expanded into broader sales and marketing execution automation. It was founded in 2020 by Paul Yacoubian (CEO) and Chris Lu (CTO), who met and worked together for roughly five years at the ESO Fund, where they helped employees at private, pre-IPO companies such as Stripe and Palantir secure financing to exercise their stock options; that background in venture-adjacent finance rather than a marketing or AI research pedigree is a notable origin detail for the company. Copy.ai is headquartered in Memphis, Tennessee, an atypical location for a venture-backed AI startup compared to the more common San Francisco Bay Area base. The company raised approximately $13.9-19.8 million in total funding across several rounds: a $2.9 million seed round in March 2021 led by Craft Ventures, an $11 million Series A in October 2021 led by Wing Venture Capital with participation from Sequoia Capital, Tiger Global, and angel investor Elad Gil, and a further $3 million convertible note round in November 2023 led by K5 Global Technology. Copy.ai grew rapidly in its early years, reportedly reaching hundreds of thousands of users and multi-million-dollar annual recurring revenue by leveraging product-led growth and a generous free tier. In a major corporate development, Copy.ai was acquired by Fullcast — a revenue operations (RevOps) and go-to-market platform company that also owns Ebsta, Atrium, and Commissionly — in October 2025, with the stated strategic goal of building a unified, AI-native 'Plan-to-Pay' GTM platform spanning sales planning, forecasting, execution, and compensation; Copy.ai's AI content and workflow capabilities are being integrated into Fullcast's Propel product line as the AI execution layer of that broader suite. This acquisition marks a shift for Copy.ai from an independent, venture-backed startup to a component of a larger RevOps software portfolio, which is a materially important fact for any buyer evaluating Copy.ai's long-term roadmap and standalone product direction. Functionally, Copy.ai's platform centers on three pillars: a chat-based AI writing interface for generating marketing copy, sales outreach, and other business content on demand; 'Brand Voice' training, which lets teams feed the AI examples of their own writing so generated content matches a consistent tone and style; and workflow automation, which chains together AI generation steps with data inputs and integrations to automate multi-step GTM tasks such as personalized outbound sequences, proposal generation, and content repurposing at scale, rather than requiring a human to manually prompt the AI for each individual piece of content. The platform supports access to multiple large language models depending on plan tier, and it offers API access and bulk workflow execution on its higher tiers for teams that want to embed Copy.ai's generation capabilities into their own internal tools or pipelines. Copy.ai's pricing follows a classic SaaS freemium model. The Free plan includes 2,000 words of chat generation per month, access to the core chat interface, training for one Brand Voice, and 200 workflow automation credits, with no credit card required to sign up. The Starter (sometimes marketed as Pro) plan costs $49 per month billed monthly (roughly $36 per month if billed annually) for a single seat, unlocking unlimited chat word generation, unlimited chat projects, and access to the platform's full roster of supported LLMs. The Team plan is priced around $249 per month (or roughly $2,232 billed annually), adding unlimited word generation, priority support, and features suited to multi-person marketing and sales teams collaborating on shared workflows. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with a guided implementation ('jumpstart'), API access and bulk workflow execution, 20-plus technology integrations, unlimited customizable workflows, and a dedicated account and support team for larger GTM organizations. Copy.ai's target users span solo content creators and small marketing teams using the free or Starter tiers for everyday copywriting, up through sales and RevOps teams at mid-market and enterprise companies using the Team and Enterprise tiers to automate outbound sequences, proposal generation, and other repeatable GTM content workflows. In the competitive landscape, Copy.ai is most frequently compared to Jasper, Writesonic, Rytr, and, more recently, general-purpose AI assistants like ChatGPT and Claude that have eroded the differentiation of dedicated AI copywriting tools; its post-acquisition positioning under Fullcast increasingly differentiates it by embedding AI writing directly into a broader sales execution and RevOps stack (alongside Ebsta's data intelligence and Atrium's analytics) rather than competing purely as a standalone copywriting tool. For a SaaS directory audience, Copy.ai should be described as an AI writing and GTM workflow automation platform now operating as part of Fullcast's RevOps product suite, valuable both to individual users seeking AI-assisted content creation and to sales/marketing organizations seeking to automate multi-step outbound and content workflows at scale. Prior to its acquisition, Copy.ai had reported reaching hundreds of thousands of registered users and multi-million-dollar annual recurring revenue, growth it attributed to an aggressive product-led growth strategy centered on its free tier and viral, template-driven content workflows shared across marketing communities. The Fullcast acquisition explicitly bundles Copy.ai alongside Fullcast's existing RevOps acquisitions — Ebsta for data intelligence, Atrium for sales analytics, and Commissionly for incentive compensation — positioning the combined 'Plan-to-Pay' suite to compete with larger RevOps and sales-engagement platforms such as Outreach, Salesloft, and Clari, which increasingly also embed generative AI content features natively rather than relying on point solutions like standalone AI copywriting tools.

enrichment.seo_title: Coral by Vox Media: Open-Source Newsroom Commenting
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coral is an open-source commenting and community platform for newsrooms, self-hostable for free or offered as managed hosting by Vox Media.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Coral, commonly known historically as The Coral Project and now branded as Coral by Vox Media, is an open-source content moderation and commenting platform purpose-built for newsrooms and publishers rather than being a generic, off-the-shelf commenting widget. The project was announced in June 2014 as a collaborative initiative between the Mozilla Foundation, The New York Times, and The Washington Post, funded by a grant from the John S. and James L. Knight Foundation, with the explicit goal of rebuilding online comment sections to be more constructive, better moderated, and more useful for both readers and journalists at a time when many publishers were shutting down or restricting comments due to abuse and moderation costs. The project initially developed three distinct tools: 'Trust,' aimed at giving news organizations data-driven ways to identify their best and most trustworthy commenters; 'Ask,' a customizable survey tool for curating conversation and gathering audience input beyond simple comment threads; and 'Talk,' the flagship commenting software designed to look and feel like a modern, thoughtfully moderated comment box rather than a typical unmoderated forum. In January 2019, Vox Media acquired The Coral Project, and the product has since been developed and commercially operated under the 'Coral by Vox Media' brand, with its open-source code hosted on GitHub under the coralproject organization (the flagship 'Talk' repository). Coral's dual-license, open-core-style business model is central to understanding it: the core commenting and moderation software is available as open source and can be downloaded, self-hosted, and run by any publisher at no licensing cost, giving smaller or budget-constrained newsrooms full access to the underlying technology; alternatively, publishers can pay Vox Media for a managed, fully hosted SaaS version of Coral along with implementation services, CMS integration, single sign-on (SSO) integration, ongoing platform maintenance, and strategic partnership support such as engagement strategy consulting and moderation team training. Pricing for the hosted and supported tiers is entirely custom and quote-based — Coral's public pricing page does not list fixed dollar figures, instead directing prospective publisher customers to submit a contact form so the Coral team can scope a package based on traffic volume, comment volume, integration complexity, and the level of support and consulting required. Functionally, Coral's differentiators center on moderation tooling designed specifically for the pressures newsroom comment sections face: AI-assisted comment moderation that helps human moderators triage and prioritize potentially problematic comments rather than reviewing every comment manually, tools to surface and highlight high-quality or 'best' comments to reward constructive engagement, configurable comment stream settings (such as requiring pre-moderation, limiting comment frequency, or closing comments after a set period), integration with CMS platforms so comments appear natively on article pages, and support for anonymous or registered commenting depending on a publisher's editorial policy. Coral is reported to be used by a wide range of publishers globally, including outlets such as The Wall Street Journal, The Washington Post, and The Globe and Mail, and Coral or its earlier Coral Project incarnation has been cited as running comment sections in roughly 30 countries and more than 20 languages, reflecting its adoption well beyond its founding US newsroom partners. Coral's target users are digital newsrooms and publishers of essentially any size — from large national outlets with dedicated moderation staff to small local or nonprofit newsrooms with limited budgets — who want to run reader comments and community engagement without either outsourcing entirely to a generic, ad-supported commenting widget (such as Disqus) or building custom moderation infrastructure from scratch. In the competitive landscape, Coral is most often compared to Disqus (a much larger, generic commenting platform with an advertising-driven free tier), OpenWeb, Viafoura, and various in-house or CMS-native commenting systems; Coral's key differentiation is its journalism-specific design philosophy, its open-source availability for self-hosting (which none of the major generic commenting SaaS competitors offer), and its nonprofit/mission-driven origin story rooted in improving the health of public discourse around news content rather than maximizing pageviews or ad impressions from comment sections. Notable facts include Coral's unusual origin as a foundation-funded, multi-organization collaborative research project rather than a typical venture-backed startup, its acquisition by Vox Media in 2019 which shifted it from a grant-funded nonprofit initiative to a commercially operated product line within a media company, and its continued commitment to keeping the core software open source even after that commercialization. For a SaaS directory audience, Coral should be positioned as the leading open-source, newsroom-specific commenting and community platform, appealing strongly to publishers who value editorial-grade moderation tooling, want the option to self-host for free, or need a vendor whose mission is explicitly aligned with healthy journalism-adjacent discourse rather than general-purpose engagement/ad metrics. Vox Media's own portfolio of digital publications, including The Verge, SB Nation, Eater, and New York Magazine, gives Coral's team direct, first-party operating experience running large-scale, high-traffic comment communities, which the company leverages as a credibility signal when selling the managed hosting and consulting side of the business to external newsroom customers. The original three-tool structure from The Coral Project era — Trust, Ask, and Talk — has since been consolidated into the unified Coral product, with Talk's commenting and moderation functionality remaining the core of what publishers deploy today. Coral's emphasis on reducing moderator burden through AI-assisted triage reflects a broader industry trend of newsrooms needing to do more comment moderation with smaller, cost-constrained community teams, which the platform's tooling is explicitly designed to address.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Coral (formerly The Coral Project) is an open-source commenting and community platform built specifically for newsrooms. It was launched in 2014 as a collaboration between Mozilla, The New York Times, and The Washington Post, funded by the Knight Foundation, and was acquired by Vox Media in 2019, which now develops and operates it as 'Coral by Vox Media.'

The flagship product, originally called 'Talk,' is designed to make comment sections more constructive and manageable for journalists, in contrast to unmoderated or ad-driven generic commenting widgets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Coral provides AI-assisted moderation tooling to help human moderators triage comments, highlights high-quality comments to reward constructive engagement, and offers configurable comment stream settings such as pre-moderation and comment-frequency limits.

It integrates with newsroom CMS platforms and supports single sign-on, and is used by publishers including The Wall Street Journal, The Washington Post, and The Globe and Mail across roughly 30 countries and 20+ languages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Coral's core commenting software is open source and free to self-host for any publisher willing to run and maintain it themselves.

Publishers who want managed hosting, CMS/SSO integration, implementation support, or strategic consulting pay Vox Media custom, quote-based pricing scoped to their traffic and support needs — there is no fixed published price list.

faqs.question: Is Coral free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Coral's core commenting software is open source and free to self-host. Managed hosting, support, and integration services from Vox Media are priced on a custom, quote basis.

faqs.question: Who created Coral (The Coral Project)?
faqs.answer: Coral was launched in 2014 as a collaboration between the Mozilla Foundation, The New York Times, and The Washington Post, funded by the Knight Foundation, and was acquired by Vox Media in 2019.

faqs.question: Which publishers use Coral?
faqs.answer: Coral is used by publishers including The Wall Street Journal, The Washington Post, and The Globe and Mail, among others across roughly 30 countries.

faqs.question: Is this the same as Coral Protocol or other 'Coral' products?
faqs.answer: No, this Coral is the open-source newsroom commenting platform originally called The Coral Project, unrelated to other unrelated software products that also use the name Coral.

faqs.question: Does Coral use AI for moderation?
faqs.answer: Yes, Coral uses AI-assisted tooling to help human moderators triage and prioritize comments that may need review.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Coral?
faqs.answer: Yes, Coral's open-source code is available on GitHub and can be self-installed and run on a publisher's own infrastructure at no software cost.

enrichment.short_description: Coralogix is a full-stack observability and log analytics platform offering logs, metrics, traces, and cost-optimization tooling on a usage-based pricing model.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coralogix is a full-stack observability platform for logs, metrics, and traces with unit-based pricing and TCO Optimizer tools to cut monitoring costs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Coralogix is a full-stack observability and log analytics platform that gives engineering and DevOps teams a unified system for collecting, analyzing, and alerting on logs, metrics, and distributed traces, with a particular emphasis on cost optimization at scale. The company was founded in 2014 by Ariel Assaraf, who serves as CEO, and Yoni Farin, who serves as CTO; Assaraf has described the company's origin as growing out of roughly four years of his own frustration managing unmanageable, sprawling log data before deciding to build a better log analytics platform. Coralogix is headquartered in Ramat Gan, within the greater Tel Aviv area of Israel, and maintains additional global offices in Boston and San Francisco in the United States, Dublin, London, Berlin, Bucharest, and Gurgaon and Bangalore in India, reflecting a globally distributed go-to-market and engineering footprint typical of scaled Israeli enterprise software companies. As of mid-2026, Coralogix employs roughly 500 people worldwide. The company has raised approximately $550 million in total funding, with its most recent and largest round being a $200 million Series F announced in June 2026, co-led by Advent International, CPPIB (Canada Pension Plan Investment Board), and Greenfield Partners, with additional participation from Brighton Park Capital; that round was explicitly framed around scaling Coralogix's observability platform to meet the demands of AI-era infrastructure monitoring. Earlier funding milestones included a $142 million Series D round in 2022 and a $115 million Series E round in 2025. The company reports serving more than 5,000 customers globally, including large enterprise names such as IBM, Tradeweb, and JFrog, and states that its revenue grew more than 60% in the year preceding the Series F raise. Architecturally, Coralogix processes petabytes of production observability data daily across eight global regions, ingesting logs, metrics, and traces from customers' applications and infrastructure and routing that data through configurable pipelines rather than treating all observability data identically. Its signature differentiator is the 'TCO Optimizer' (Total Cost of Ownership Optimizer), a feature that lets teams define policies routing incoming data to different processing and storage tiers based on its business value and query needs — for example, sending high-priority data destined for frequent, real-time search into a 'Frequent Search' tier, sending lower-priority data used mainly for periodic monitoring dashboards into a cheaper 'Monitoring' tier, sending compliance-only data that's rarely queried into an even cheaper 'Compliance' archive tier, or blocking genuinely low-value data at ingestion entirely — all without losing the ability to query that data later if needed, and all without the traditional trade-off of either paying full price to index everything or discarding data outright. This pipeline-based approach to cost management is Coralogix's primary competitive differentiator against observability incumbents that generally charge a flat, high per-GB indexing rate regardless of how frequently the data is actually queried. Coralogix's pricing model reflects this architecture: rather than per-seat, per-host, or flat per-GB pricing, the company uses a unit-based system where one 'unit' is priced at roughly $1.50 and represents a variable quantity of data depending on data type and destination tier — for instance, roughly 1.3 GB of Frequent Search-tier logs might equal one unit, while as much as 3 GB of Monitoring-tier logs might also equal one unit, because Monitoring-tier data costs less per gigabyte to process and store. Customers purchase a committed daily or monthly unit quota, and Coralogix offers a 'Pay-As-You-Go' overage mechanism that lets ingestion continue automatically up to roughly double the committed daily quota when a customer is alerted at 80% utilization, avoiding hard data-loss cutoffs during traffic spikes. There is no permanent free tier, but Coralogix offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, providing full feature access and a daily quota of 8 units for evaluation purposes; published G2 reviews from smaller businesses note that the unit-based pricing model, while flexible, can feel complex or comparatively expensive at low data volumes, making Coralogix a better fit for mid-market and enterprise customers with meaningful observability data volume than for very small teams or hobbyist projects. Coralogix's target users are DevOps, SRE, and platform engineering teams at growth-stage and enterprise companies that are either outgrowing the cost structure of legacy observability vendors or need more granular control over what they pay for different classes of monitoring data, particularly as AI-driven infrastructure generates dramatically more observability data volume than traditional application stacks. In the competitive observability landscape, Coralogix is most frequently compared to Datadog, Splunk, Sumo Logic, New Relic, and Grafana Labs/Grafana Cloud, with its core differentiation being the TCO Optimizer's granular, policy-based cost control versus the largely flat or tiered indexing pricing common among competitors like Datadog and Splunk, which has historically driven customer complaints about unpredictable, rapidly scaling observability bills. Notable facts include Coralogix's rapid fundraising acceleration in 2025-2026 explicitly tied to positioning itself as observability infrastructure for the 'age of AI,' its unusually broad global office footprint for a company of its size, and its stated 60%+ year-over-year revenue growth ahead of its Series F raise. For a SaaS directory audience, Coralogix should be positioned as a cost-conscious, full-stack observability platform particularly well suited to engineering organizations with substantial and growing log, metric, and trace volumes who want granular control over observability spend rather than a flat, unpredictable per-GB bill.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Coralogix is a full-stack observability platform for logs, metrics, and distributed traces, founded in 2014 by Ariel Assaraf (CEO) and Yoni Farin (CTO) and headquartered in Ramat Gan, in the greater Tel Aviv area of Israel, with additional offices across the US, Europe, and India.

The company has raised roughly $550 million in total funding, including a $200 million Series F in June 2026 co-led by Advent International, CPPIB, and Greenfield Partners, and reports more than 5,000 customers worldwide, including IBM, Tradeweb, and JFrog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Coralogix's signature capability is the TCO Optimizer, which routes incoming observability data to different pricing and storage tiers (Frequent Search, Monitoring, Compliance/Archive, or block) based on its business value, avoiding the all-or-nothing indexing cost of legacy observability tools.
The platform processes petabytes of data daily across eight global regions and covers logs, metrics, and traces in a single unified system rather than requiring separate tools for each data type.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Coralogix uses unit-based pricing at roughly $1.50 per unit, where the amount of data represented by a unit varies depending on which processing tier the data is routed to.

There is no permanent free plan, but a 14-day free trial with no credit card required offers full feature access and a daily quota of 8 units; a Pay-As-You-Go overage option lets ingestion continue up to double the daily committed quota during usage spikes.

faqs.question: How does Coralogix pricing work?
faqs.answer: Coralogix uses a unit-based pricing model where one unit costs roughly $1.50 and represents a variable amount of data depending on data type and which processing tier (Frequent Search, Monitoring, Compliance) it's routed to.

faqs.question: Does Coralogix have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No permanent free plan is available, but Coralogix offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required and full feature access up to a daily quota of 8 units.

faqs.question: What is the TCO Optimizer?
faqs.answer: The TCO Optimizer is Coralogix's cost-control feature that lets teams route data to different pricing/storage tiers based on its business value, so low-priority data costs less to store while high-priority data remains fully searchable.

faqs.question: Who founded Coralogix?
faqs.answer: Coralogix was founded in 2014 by CEO Ariel Assaraf and CTO Yoni Farin and is headquartered in Ramat Gan, in the greater Tel Aviv area of Israel.

faqs.question: What data types does Coralogix support?
faqs.answer: Coralogix supports logs, metrics, and distributed traces in a single unified observability platform.

faqs.question: Who are Coralogix's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Coralogix is commonly compared to Datadog, Splunk, Sumo Logic, New Relic, and Grafana Cloud in the observability market.
